content.text: "Biometric-gated retrieval" in the context of Claude on Android means retrieving a short-lived session token that was encrypted with a hardware-backed Keystore key. The biometric prompt proves the user's presence before the TEE allows the key to be used for decryption. The decrypted session token is then sent to your backend proxy, which holds the actual Claude API key server-side. The API key itself is never on the device; biometrics protect access to the token that reaches your proxy.

Never store the Anthropic API key in the Android Keystore or anywhere on the device. The Keystore protects the encryption key for your session token. The session token authenticates to your backend. The backend calls Claude. Three separate layers — each protecting the one below it.

attrs.alt: Sequence diagram: biometric prompt authenticates CryptoObject, decrypted session token reaches backend proxy, Claude API called server-side, SSE response streams back
attrs.title: Biometric-Gated Session Token Flow for Claude API

content.text: Before showing a biometric prompt, check whether the device has usable biometric hardware and whether the user has enrolled credentials.
returns one of five status codes. Handle all of them — silently assuming success leads to a crash or an opaque failure dialog.

import androidx.biometric.BiometricManager
import androidx.biometric.BiometricManager.Authenticators.BIOMETRIC_STRONG
import androidx.biometric.BiometricManager.Authenticators.DEVICE_CREDENTIAL

sealed interface BiometricCapability {
    object Available            : BiometricCapability
    object NoHardware          : BiometricCapability
    object HardwareUnavailable : BiometricCapability
    object NoneEnrolled        : BiometricCapability
    object SecurityUpdateRequired : BiometricCapability
}

fun checkBiometricCapability(context: Context): BiometricCapability {
    val manager = BiometricManager.from(context)
    return when (manager.canAuthenticate(BIOMETRIC_STRONG or DEVICE_CREDENTIAL)) {
        BiometricManager.BIOMETRIC_SUCCESS              -> BiometricCapability.Available
        BiometricManager.BIOMETRIC_ERROR_NO_HARDWARE   -> BiometricCapability.NoHardware
        BiometricManager.BIOMETRIC_ERROR_HW_UNAVAILABLE -> BiometricCapability.HardwareUnavailable
        BiometricManager.BIOMETRIC_ERROR_NONE_ENROLLED -> BiometricCapability.NoneEnrolled
        BiometricManager.BIOMETRIC_ERROR_SECURITY_UPDATE_REQUIRED -> BiometricCapability.SecurityUpdateRequired
        else -> BiometricCapability.HardwareUnavailable
    }
}
is common on emulators and freshly-wiped devices. Respond by showing a rationale dialog and optionally deep-linking to the system enrollment screen with
on older API levels. Do not simply throw — the app must remain functional for users without biometrics if you provide a fallback PIN-only flow.
The critical design decision is using a
: it ties the biometric result to a specific, already-initialised
. The TEE will only allow the Cipher to complete
if the authentication happened with that exact Cipher object. A plain auth prompt (without CryptoObject) does not gate Keystore operations — it only calls your callback.
The biometric callback fires on the main executor, so you can update

directly inside it. Model each auth outcome as a UI state so the composable or Fragment can react precisely — show a loading indicator during auth, a rationale screen on key invalidation, and restore the idle chat input on cancellation.
receives a numeric error code. Map the most important ones to user-visible actions rather than generic error messages.
Restore Idle state silently — user dismissed intentionally
Too many failures; show message: "Try again in 30 seconds"
Device PIN required to unlock; redirect to re-login flow
Deep-link to enrollment settings or offer PIN-only path
Fallback to PIN-only or disable the feature for this device

After a successful backend login, encrypt the session token with a fresh Keystore AES key and persist the ciphertext + IV. On subsequent launches, only the IV is needed upfront to initialise the Cipher for the biometric flow — the ciphertext decryption happens inside the auth callback.
will generate a new key on the next
call. This ensures biometric-enrollment changes do not leave stale keys in hardware — the new key is tied to the current biometric set.
Using a plain BiometricPrompt without CryptoObject.
does not gate Keystore operations. An attacker who hooks
in a compromised process can call your decrypt function directly. The
binding is what makes biometrics meaningful for Keystore operations.
Calling Cipher.init() on the main thread before the prompt.
with a Keystore key is a blocking TEE call (10–200 ms). Move it to
(which must be on the main thread) after init completes.
If the user adds a fingerprint after the key was created, the key is silently deleted by the system. The exception is thrown at
, not at decryption time. Catch it there, call
, and redirect to the login screen.
Treating onAuthenticationFailed as a terminal error.

fires for each single rejected attempt while the prompt stays visible. It is not an error; the system already shows the user a retry message. Do not dismiss the dialog or update your state in this callback.
Using BIOMETRIC_WEAK authenticators for Keystore operations.
Class 2 (weak) biometrics (some face-unlock implementations) are not accepted by Keystore-backed operations — only
alone in the authenticator flags will produce a runtime exception when you pass a CryptoObject.
Biometric gating protects a short-lived session token, not the Claude API key. The API key lives server-side. The session token proves client identity to your proxy, which then calls Claude.
